summary:
Screening may improve perceived survival times through lead time bias rather than reducing actual mortality. True improvement requires assessing mortality rates instead of survival durations because early detection does not reduce fatality risk—it only delays symptomatic presentation. {\boxed{B}} Based on the retrieved evidence, I disagree with relying solely on survival rates in this observational case-control study. While screen-detected patients appear to have better 5-year survival due to earlier detection, this reflects lead time bias—the increase in observed survival time merely shifts diagnosis forward without altering actual mortality risk. Mortality rates properly capture the true reduction in death incidence and thus correctly assess whether screening improves patient outcomes.

The summary provided by Agent2 focuses primarily on identifying **lead time bias** as a critical flaw in interpreting survival data from screening studies — emphasizing that increased observed survival duration results from earlier diagnosis but doesn't imply reduced mortality. This leads directly to recommending assessment based on **mortality rates**, which accurately measure real reductions in death risk despite no biological change in tumor progression.
